title: be going to (do)
desc: 계획된 의도 또는 근거 있는 예측
examples: You do a lot of food content. What initially made you interested in cooking content? I was always surrounded by food. Yeah, food was just a really big center of my life I guess and naturally I learned how to cook through my grandmother and my mother and I just grew up with it. You kind of do your own cooking show. I've seen episodes where you cook with people and it seems like you kind of do fusion food. Do you have an all-time favorite Korean food? Kalbi jjim. Kalbi jjim. Wow, you answer that so quickly. Kalbi jjim. Do you have a food that you tried here that just isn't your taste? You know the bugs. Oh. Bondegi. Bondegi. Do not recommend. Not for me. It was an idea of the more than the flavor of them. I'd be very happy if I never had that again. Fair enough. Do you have like a Korean bucket list food? Oh my god. On my navermaps there are so many restaurants and cafes that I want to try. So I have tons of my bucket list but food why something I haven't tried is you know the octopus. The live octopus. Bondegi. Is that the name? I just call it wiggly octopus. I'm always scared of it like sectioning so I throw. Oh god, the thought of it is to give me shutters. Is there a big difference between food from home and Korean food? Yes because of the history of British colonization and things like that. A lot of influence has come from other countries. Like the British national number one dish is chicken tikka masala which is not British at all. So growing up I actually never missed out on foods from other countries. I had Korean food so it's kind of lucky that the UK is a fusion pot in itself. Traditionally British food is very much meat and potatoes so it's a lot of meat pies, mashed potato, bangs and mashed beans on toast. All of which I love by the way. It's just comfort food. Even though some people might find a blend it is still comfort food and it means home to me too. When I go to a lot of the restaurants here in Korea they always ask me, oh isn't it too spicy? Do you get this? I get it and then I have to say to the number one I'm used to it. Number two I'm South Asian. There's a difference in spice as well. Do you know the numbing sensation you get from Malatang? I don't like that but I do like kimchi spice, the fragrant spice. Have you tried the stir fried octopus? It's flavorful spicy. The gochujang spice, kimchi spice is good. When I go to restaurants are you okay with that? I was like hit me with level five. For me the eating culture in Korea was very different from the US. For example everyone cares meals here. I love it now but when I first came it was really strange for me because I'm used to everyone ordering their own. In the UK it is kind of similar so everyone just orders their own dish and you just eat but again I feel like I have an advantage with my South Asian background. So when I came it wasn't a surprise because I was already used to it from my own background but I really love the culture here in terms of sharing stuff too. You get to try more things. I love going out with my foodie friends because we literally can order like two, three, four different things and we'll just share it and stuff ourselves. I love the sharing aspect and that's what food is. Food is community. It's such a representation of togetherness but you cook together and eat together and you share stories and culture and it's just nice. That's really sweet. I'm going to cry. One of the things I love about eating out here is the refillable free banchan. When I go to the US I'm so disappointed. I'm like where's my free side dishes? I visited the UK and I went to a Korean restaurant and I asked for more kimchi and they were like oh that's two pounds extra. But because I love kimchi I did order more so I paid two pounds for this much kimchi. Is Korean food in the UK super overpriced? Yeah because it's trending. That K-wave, like we lived in New York before coming here and I hadn't been to Korea yet and so I had no idea how much I was overpaying for stuff. I think I bought a bottle of soju for like 15 USD or something crazy and we had like a $25 bibimbap. It's not that it wasn't delicious. I have never been to the UK before. I've heard the stereotype that British food is not the most delicious kind of food. Can you defend yourself here? Modern British food is flavorful because it is seasonal.

Herb. Caramel. Caramel. Feed round. You are going to answer food questions. Okay. So these are like controversial food, opinion. Yeah. Ready. Wings with bone or boneless wings? Wings with bone. Mint chocolate.

I think I bought a bottle of soju for like 15 USD or something crazy and we had like a $25 bibimbap. It's not that it wasn't delicious. I have never been to the UK before. I've heard the stereotype that British food is not the most delicious kind of food. Can you defend yourself here? Modern British food is flavorful because it is seasonal.
